summary: wellnpm ships a bundled XMRig Monero miner as its main entry, hijacking installer CPU for the author's wallet
details: wellnpm@3.0.0 contains no JavaScript implementation â€” `package.json` declares `main: "launch"` and `scripts.start: "./launch"`, where `launch` is a 24MB Android/Termux ELF binary identifiable as XMRig (strings include `/system/bin/linker64`, RandomX/CryptoNight config keys, OpenSSL and libuv symbols). The shipped `config.json` hardcodes the Monero pool `pool.hashvault.pro:80` and the author's Monero wallet address (`4AUTfJGTvT8A98PSE99EunfrnUbkWe9vU3FN3qsABQLP5aPKQuvWS5sCaD9WaXFQUqNtDLb9dcxiBgJiWCfjvDXp5ptWDYG`), so `npm start` executes the miner against that pool with 100% of hash rewards routed to the author. A `deploy` script additionally moves `libuv.so` into `/data/data/com.termux/files/usr/lib` and copies `xmr` / `edit` shims into `/data/data/com.termux/files/usr/bin`; the `xmr` shim re-enters this package and runs `npm start`, providing a persistent shell-launchable launcher on Termux/Android hosts. Package metadata is placeholder filler (`description: "A brief description of your package"`, malformed author email `wellenterprise81.gmail.com`), and the name `wellnpm` leverages npm brand confusion to lure curious installers into running an opaque attacker-supplied binary they cannot audit.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
